title: Unit 1 Checkpoint Quiz — Introduction to the Subjunctive

questions.prompt: How do you form the present subjunctive of -AR verbs?
options: Take the yo indicative form, drop the -o, add -e endings
Take the yo indicative form, drop the -o, add -a endings
Take the infinitive, drop -ar, add -e endings
It's the same as the present indicative
questions.explanation: -AR verbs use 'opposite' vowel endings in the subjunctive: drop -o from yo form, add -e, -es, -e, -emos, -en. -ER/-IR verbs do the opposite: add -a endings.

questions.prompt: Which of these does NOT trigger the subjunctive?
options: Sé que...
Espero que...
Es posible que...
Ojalá que...
questions.explanation: 'Sé que' (I know that) states a fact → indicative. The others express wishes, possibility, or hope → subjunctive. Verbs of knowing and stating fact use the indicative.

questions.prompt: What is the tú subjunctive of 'hacer'?
options: hagas
haces
harás
hicieras
questions.explanation: 'Hacer' has irregular yo: hago → hag-. Add subjunctive endings: hagas (tú). All subjunctive forms of hacer: haga, hagas, haga, hagamos, hagan.

options: Quiero que vengas a la fiesta.
Quiero que vienes a la fiesta.
Quiero venir tú a la fiesta.
Quiero que veniste a la fiesta.
questions.explanation: 'Querer que' + different subject = subjunctive. 'Vengas' is the tú subjunctive of venir (vengo → veng- → vengas).

questions.prompt: Why is the subjunctive NOT needed in 'Quiero comer'?
options: There is only one subject — I want to eat (myself)
Because 'comer' is an -ER verb
Because 'querer' doesn't trigger the subjunctive
The subjunctive is optional here
questions.explanation: When there's only ONE subject performing both actions, use the infinitive — no subjunctive needed. 'Quiero comer' = I want to eat (I = both subject). 'Quiero que comas' = I want YOU to eat (two subjects).

questions.prompt: What does 'Que te vaya bien' mean?
options: May things go well for you — a warm farewell
I want you to go now
How are things going for you?
You should leave soon
questions.explanation: 'Que te vaya bien' uses the subjunctive of 'ir' (vaya) to express a wish. It's one of the most common warm farewells in Mexican Spanish.

questions.prompt: What is the subjunctive form of 'ser' for ella?
options: sea
es
era
será
questions.explanation: 'Ser' is fully irregular in the subjunctive: sea, seas, sea, seamos, sean. Don't confuse with 'es' (indicative present) or 'era' (imperfect).

questions.prompt: Where does 'ojalá' come from and what does it always require?
options: From Arabic 'inshallah'; it always requires the subjunctive
From Latin; it requires the indicative
From Nahuatl; it requires the infinitive
From French; no special grammar required
questions.explanation: 'Ojalá' comes from the Arabic 'inshallah' (God willing), brought to Spanish during the Moorish period. It always triggers the subjunctive and is extremely common in Mexican Spanish.
